result:
The first thing that strikes me is how arbitrary the number is: one thousand words. A millennium of syllables, a currency of language. I could spend them all on a single blade of grass if I wanted—describe the way dew trembles along its edge like a tightrope walker rehearsing for a dawn performance no one will applaud. Or I could ticker-tape them across the sky, a fireworks show of nouns and verbs that briefly rearrange the constellations before the dark reclaims its own. The choice feels monumental, like standing at a buffet where every dish is a possible future and the plate is only so big.

I once read that the average person speaks roughly sixteen thousand words a day. That means this block of text is about six percent of a daily voice, a vocal cord’s sliver of existence. And yet here we are, you and I, trafficking in this sliver as though it might hold the entire hourglass. Maybe because we know that sometimes a solitary sentence—“I’m leaving,” “It’s benign,” “We’re expecting”—can tilt the axis of a life more decisively than libraries of noise. So perhaps a thousand curated words are enough to mint a small miracle, or at least to smuggle a seed across the border of your attention.

Let me tell you about the time my grandmother tried to mail a piece of sunshine. She was seventy-eight, living in a Michigan town so small the welcome sign also said “come back soon.” I was nine, spending August in her creaking farmhouse where the clocks refused to agree. She took a Mason jar, held it up to the window at high noon, and screwed the lid tight, convinced she’d captured something migratory. Then she wrapped the jar in yesterday’s obituaries, nested it in a shoebox, and addressed the package to my cousin stationed in Kodiak, Alaska, where the sun had apparently gone AWOL that summer. The postmaster—who was also the mayor, volunteer fire chief, and lead alto in the Lutheran choir—accepted the parcel with the solemnity of a diplomat receiving nuclear codes. Two weeks later we got a letter: the jar arrived intact, but “the sunshine seems to have evaporated in transit,” my cousin wrote, “though the glass still smells like cut hay and thunder.” Grandma set that letter on the windowsill, and every evening the setting sun lit the paper as though confirming the story’s postscript: some things survive the journey even when they look like nothing.

I think about that jar when I fret over semantics—how many photons fit inside a metaphor, whether adverbs are parasites or pollinators, whether any paragraph can outrun our congenital loneliness. The experiment proved you can’t ship photons, but you can transmit the gesture of trying, and that residue lingers longer than physics allows. Words behave like that: once released, they orbit the people who need them, even when their dictionary definitions have long since combusted. I still keep a mental picture of an invisible label on that jar: Contents—approximate warmth. Handle hopefully.

Midway through this word-count, doubt taps me on the shoulder like a process server. He’s wearing the same cheap tie every internal critic owns, the one patterned with repetitions of “who do you think you are.” He whispers statistics: average reading speed 250 words per minute, dwindling attention span shorter than a goldfish’s diary entry. Why bother? But I remember a night train to Prague when a stranger handed me half a page torn from Milosz, crooked lines about how love means learning to look at the same face for years without turning it into an ideology. I read those fragments until the paper went soft from the heat of my thumbs, and by the time the Danube appeared outside the window, something in my chest had been renamed. Four minutes of reading, lifelong echo. Maybe these thousand words are that torn half-page for somebody scrolling under fluorescent lights at 2 a.m., waiting for lab results or a lover’s text. Maybe you.

So let’s get practical. If words are currency, we should budget. No impulse buying on adjectives ending in -ous. Coupons clipped for conjunctions—fewer buts, more ands. Invest in concrete nouns: shovel, thunderclap, persimmon. Avoid speculative bubbles of abstract jargon; “actualization” deflates faster than a carnival balloon. Sprinkle tactile verbs: scuff, simmer, solder. And always keep a spare pronoun in the glove compartment; you never know when you’ll need to switch points of view to avoid a head-on collision with yourself.

Now, an inventory of emergencies these words could provision. 1) The stalled elevator: read these lines aloud, each syllable a floor you pass on the way to breathing normally. 2) The obligatory wedding toast when you’ve lost the notecards: commence with “love is a Mason jar of sunshine,” end before the divorce lawyers at table seven start billing by the hour. 3) The insomniac hour when your thoughts resemble untied shoelaces, trip-able: let these paragraphs be the night-light that doesn’t care how ugly your crying face is. I’m not saying language is a Swiss army knife; it’s more like a yo-yo—useless for survival, absurdly consoling once you trust its up-and-down.

Let’s spend a few more coins on sound. Listen to the k in “ink,” the way it closes like a miniature trunk lid. Say “cellar door” just to watch the vowels curtsy. Notice how “hush” is onomatopoeic in every dialect the world has ever muttered. The ear is a refugee camp for noises that outgrew their original mouths; give sanctuary to the hiss of retreating tires, the glottal stop of a kiss interrupted by laughter. A thousand words can be a xylophone if you let them strike the mallet of your breath.

We’re approaching the ledger’s final lines, the cash drawer about to ring shut. I could deploy a moral, something bumper-stickerable like “kindness is free, sprinkle that stuff everywhere,” but the price of cliché is inflationary; by the time you get home it shrinks in the wash. Instead, let me offer a single comma-shaped moment: pause. Right here, between this sentence and the next, inhale as though you’re about to submerge. Feel the lungs expand, the tiny alveoli blooming like inverted umbrellas. That micro-vacation cost zero words, yet it reset the meter of your pulse. We’re so busy bartering futures we forget the treasury of now. Use your remaining heartbeats to store silence; it appreciates faster than any adjective I could broker.

Word count tells me we’re nearly broke. I have maybe forty left, small change clinking in the pocket. I’ll spend the penultimate few on thank-you, because gratitude is a bell that keeps tolling after the tollbooth is abandoned. And for the finale, a verb intransitive yet imperative: Stay. Stay open, stay unfinishing, stay sufficient unto these insufficient syllables. The world will reload itself with noise tomorrow; for now, let the last word be the hush that follows a story told in the dark, proof that something still travels when the voice has gone to ground.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
